data.title: Monday’s Economic Calendar
data.description:
10:00 AM Existing Home Sales

Existing home sales tally the number of previously constructed homes, condominiums, and co-ops in which a sale closed during the month.

6:20 PM Fed's Miran Speech

 
Federal Reserve Board Governor Stephen Miran participates in conversation before the Symposium on Building the Financial System of the 21st Century: "An Agenda for Europe and the United States"

data.title: Asia stocks retreat as Hormuz Blockade fears and stalled peace talks weigh on sentiment; oil prices spike
data.description:
Asian equity markets fell on Monday after US-Iran peace talks ended without progress, raising global growth concerns. Trump announced on Sunday that the US Navy would enforce a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, increasing tensions and oil prices.

Gold prices dropped about 2% to below $4,700 an ounce on Monday.

WTI crude futures climbed as much as 9.3% to above $105 per barrel on Monday.

The benchmark KOSPI fell nearly 1% to around 5,805 on Monday, reversing from the previous session. The Bank of Korea 

Japan (NKY:IND) fell 0.94% to below 56,500 on Monday, giving back part of last week’s gains. The Japanese yen depreciated past 159.5 per dollar on Monday, sliding for the third straight session.

China's (SHCOMP) fell 0.17% to 3,980 on Monday, while the Shenzhen Component rose 0.5% to 14,386, and the offshore yuan weakened to around 6.83 per dollar on Monday.

US President Donald Trump recently warned that countries aiding Iran's military, including China, could face economic penalties, potentially leading to tariffs of up to 50% on exports to the US.

Hong Kong (HSI) fell 1.15% to 25,600 on Monday, reversing gains from last week’s rally.

India (SENSEX) fell 1.76% to 76,007 due to higher oil prices, and the rupee fell to around 93.37 per dollar. The Asian Development Bank raised India's GDP growth forecast for FY2026-27 to 6.9% from 6.5%.

Australia (AS51) fell 0.51% to 8,907 in early deals on Monday, marking losses for the second straight session. The Australian dollar weakened to below $0.703, easing from a three-week high.

In the U.S. on Friday, all three major indexes struggled amid seemingly lower geopolitical tensions.

U.S. stock futures declined on Monday after President Donald Trump announced a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz: Dow -0.79%; S&P 500 -0.76%; Nasdaq -0.85%.

Currencies: (JPY:USD), (CNY:USD), (AUD:USD), (INR:USD), (HKD:USD), (NZD:USD).

More on Asia's markets and economy:

data.title: Trump says gas prices could stay high through November midterm elections
data.description:
• U.S. President Donald Trump said oil and gasoline prices could stay elevated through November’s midterm elections, acknowledging potential political fallout from his decision to strike Iran six weeks earlier.
  • "It could be, or the same, or maybe a little ‌bit higher, but it should be around the same," Trump, who is in Miami for the weekend, told Fox News' "Sunday Morning Futures With Maria Bartiromo" when asked whether the cost of oil and gas would be lower by the fall.
  • The average price for regular gas at U.S. service stations has exceeded $4 per gallon for most of April, according to data from GasBuddy. Average U.S. gas prices in February hovered just ​below $3 per gallon, and over the past year never exceeded $3.25 per gallon, according to GasBuddy.
  • Earlier on Sunday, Trump ⁠announced on social media that the U.S. Navy would blockade the Strait of Hormuz and intercept any ship that paid a crossing fee to Iran, after marathon talks between the ​U.S. and Iran in Pakistan over the weekend did not yield a peace deal.
  • Several hours after Trump's post, U.S. Central Command said the blockade would be limited to ships going to and from Iranian ports. Ships entering and exiting non-Iranian ports will not be stopped by U.S. forces, CENTCOM said.

data.title: Trump-linked crypto venture faces investor revolt over fund freeze allegations
data.description:
World Liberty Financial, a crypto venture linked to the Trump family, is facing backlash from investors, including billionaire supporter Justin Sun, who alleges the project quietly implemented mechanisms allowing insiders to freeze users’ funds.

Sun, who poured tens of millions of dollars into World Liberty, called the project “a trap masquerading as a door” in a post on X on Sunday.

Tron blockchain founder Sun claimed on X that the company has embedded a “backdoor blacklisting function” in its smart contract.

“This function gives the Company unilateral power to freeze, restrict, and effectively confiscate the property rights of any token holder, without notice, without cause, and without recourse,” the cryptocurrency billionaire added.

data.title: Meta builds AI version of Mark Zuckerberg for employees: FT
data.description:
• Meta (META) recently began prioritizing an AI character based on Mark Zuckerberg that can offer conversation and feedback to employees, the Financial Times reported, citing unidentified people familiar with the matter.
  • Zuckerberg is personally involved in training and testing his animated AI, the report said. Character will be trained on his mannerisms, tone, and publicly available statements, as well as recent thinking on company strategies.
  • The group has been working on developing photorealistic, AI 3D characters that users can interact with in real time.

data.title: Odds of Strait of Hormuz opening plunge on prediction markets
data.description:
The likelihood of the Strait of Hormuz being open to traffic tumbled Sunday after the U.S. moved to block Iranian ports and peace talks ended with no agreement.

On Kalshi, the odds of traffic through the Strait returning to normal before June 1 dropped from a high of 66% on Saturday to 39% Sunday evening.

On Polymarket, the odds of traffic returning to normal before the end of May fell to 38% from 65% on Saturday.

data.title: Trump is said to weigh next moves on Iran after talks collapse
data.description:
President Donald Trump is considering renewed military action against Iran, including limited strikes, following the breakdown of negotiations in Pakistan, The Wall Street Journal reported Sunday, citing officials familiar with the discussions.

A broader bombing campaign remains an option but is seen as less likely, given concerns about escalation and prolonged conflict.

The U.S. has already imposed a naval blockade on the Strait of Hormuz, a critical global energy chokepoint, while Trump continues to signal openness to diplomacy. Still, he warned of potential escalation, saying, “I would hate to do it, but it’s their water, their desalinization plans, their electric-generating plants, which are very easy to hit.”

White House officials emphasized that no final decision has been made, the Journal reported.

U.S. demands in any renewed talks include reopening the strait without tolls, ending uranium enrichment, dismantling nuclear facilities and halting support for regional proxy groups. Talks led by Vice President JD Vance collapsed after Iran refused to abandon its nuclear program.

Some advisers see the blockade as the most effective near-term strategy, aiming to pressure Iran economically by restricting oil exports. Others warn it could provoke retaliation, including missile or drone attacks on U.S. naval forces in the region.

Trump has argued that economic disruption is a necessary tradeoff to prevent Iran from obtaining nuclear weapons, even as rising energy prices and political pressure at home complicate the path forward, the Journal reported.

Dear Readers: We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Oil surges past $100 as U.S. moves to blockade Iran’s ports
data.description:
Oil prices spiked above $100 a barrel late Sunday in the United States after the White House signaled it would begin blockading Iranian ports, escalating tensions with Iran following failed negotiations.

Brent crude (CO1:COM) jumped more than 7% to about $102 a barrel, while U.S. West Texas Intermediate (CL1:COM) climbed over 8% to roughly $104. The move rattled financial markets, with futures tied to the Dow (DJI), S&P 500 (SP500) and Nasdaq (COMP:IND) all pointing lower.

The surge comes after a marathon round of talks in Pakistan between U.S. officials, led by Vice President JD Vance, and Iranian negotiators ended without agreement. Vance said Iran declined to accept key terms, including commitments tied to its nuclear program. Iranian state media countered that Washington’s demands were excessive, citing disputes over uranium enrichment and control of the strait.

President Donald Trump announced that the U.S. Navy would begin blocking vessels entering or leaving Iranian ports, warning that ships paying tolls to Tehran would not have safe passage. U.S. Central Command said the operation would begin Monday morning.

Trump also issued a stark warning, saying any Iranian attacks on U.S. forces or commercial shipping would be met with overwhelming force, while reiterating that key infrastructure targets remain vulnerable.

Ahead of the blockade, U.S. naval forces moved into the region to clear potential mines, with allies including the U.K. expected to assist.

The Strait of Hormuz, a vital artery for global energy supplies, has remained largely restricted despite a recent ceasefire. Any prolonged disruption risks tightening global supplies of oil, natural gas and fertilizers, while the immediate impact of a blockade could further constrain tanker traffic, particularly Iranian exports.

data.title: ‘Super Mario Galaxy Movie’ still in control as ‘Project Hail Mary’ holds strong
data.description:
The North American box office showed a familiar pattern over the April 10–12 weekend, with established hits continuing to dominate while a handful of newcomers carved out modest openings, according to studio estimates compiled by Comscore.

Universal (CMCSA) and Illumination’s “The Super Mario Galaxy Movie” remained firmly in first place in its second weekend, generating an estimated $69.0 million. While down 48% from its opening frame, the animated juggernaut has already amassed a towering $308.1 million domestically and continues to demonstrate global staying power.

Close behind, Amazon MGM’s (AMZN) “Project Hail Mary” maintained a strong hold in its fourth weekend, declining just 23% to $24.58 million. The Ryan Gosling-led sci-fi adaptation has now reached $256.7 million in North America and more than $510 million worldwide, signaling sustained audience interest.

A24’s “The Drama” held onto third place with $8.7 million in its sophomore outing, bringing its domestic total to just over $30.8 million. The Zendaya-Robert Pattinson pairing continues to draw audiences, particularly given its relatively modest 39% drop.

The weekend’s top new wide release, “You, Me & Tuscany,” opened in fourth place with $8.0 million across 3,151 locations. The romantic dramedy, centered on a drifting young woman finding herself in Italy, delivered a per-theater average of $2,539 and suggests a measured but not breakout start.

Disney’s (DIS) Pixar title “Hoppers” rounded out the top five with $4.1 million in its sixth weekend, pushing its domestic total to $157.1 million and reinforcing its position as one of 2026’s more reliable family performers.

Further down the chart, IFC’s horror entry “Faces of Death” debuted in sixth with $1.7 million, while Neon’s experimental thriller “Exit 8” followed in seventh with $1.4 million despite a limited 495-theater release. Specialty titles continued to find niche audiences, including Vertical’s “Hamlet” and Neon’s “The Christophers,” the latter boasting an impressive per-theater average in ultra-limited release.

Overall, the top 10 films generated a combined $120.6 million, underscoring a market still driven by a handful of dominant titles rather than broad-based breakout success.

Top 10 Films (Weekend of April 10–12, 2026)

  1. The Super Mario Galaxy Movie – $69.0M
  2. Project Hail Mary – $24.58M
  3. The Drama – $8.71M
  4. You, Me & Tuscany – $8.0M
  5. Hoppers – $4.1M
  6. Faces of Death – $1.7M
  7. Exit 8 – $1.40M
  8. Great Awakening, A – $1.27M
  9. Reminders of Him – $1.0M
  10. Ready or Not 2: Here I Come – $867K

Total (Top 10): $120,630,392

data.title: GFL is said to near $4.3B deal for Secure Waste in major expansion push
data.description:
GFL Environmental Inc. (GFL) is said to be close to acquiring Secure Waste Infrastructure Corp. (SECYF) (SES:CA) in a deal valued at more than C$6 billion ($4.33 billion), including debt, Bloomberg News reported, citing a person familiar with the talks.

The proposed transaction would be structured with about 20% cash and 80% stock, valuing Secure at roughly 11 times earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization. The implied offer price of around C$24.50 a share represents a premium of about 15% to recent trading levels, though negotiations are ongoing and could still shift.

If completed, the acquisition would significantly deepen GFL’s (GFL) presence in western Canada and expand its industrial and energy-related waste services. Secure shareholders would hold about 15% of the combined company.

GFL, led by CEO Patrick Dovigi, has aggressively grown through acquisitions, tripling its market value over the past six years. Secure, based in Calgary, generates most of its earnings from waste management, with the remainder tied to energy infrastructure such as pipelines and storage.

The company sees the deal as strategically complementary, with potential for cost efficiencies and cross-selling opportunities. It follows GFL’s recent agreement to acquire Texas-based Frontier Waste Solutions for roughly $900 million.

The companies haven’t commented publicly, and there is no guarantee the deal will be finalized.
